title: JTBD Four Forces
definition: Push, pull, anxiety, and habit — the four forces that determine whether a customer switches to a new solution.
example: A user is pushed by slow bank transfers (push) and pulled by instant payouts (pull), but anxiety about security and habit of using PayPal block switching until onboarding addresses both.
whenToUse: Evaluating why users adopt or reject a solution; designing onboarding and messaging.
frameworkSteps: Identify push of the situation (pain with status quo)
Identify pull of the new solution (attraction)
Map habits keeping them on current solution
interviewPrompts: What was happening in your life when you first thought about changing?
What almost stopped you from switching?
officeUse: Opportunity assessments, positioning, and conversion funnel analysis.

title: Job Statement
definition: A structured sentence: When [situation], I want to [motivation], so I can [expected outcome].
example: When I close a client project, I want to send a professional invoice quickly, so I can get paid without awkward follow-up emails.
whenToUse: Framing discovery insights and aligning team on user motivation without solution bias.
interviewPrompts: What were you trying to accomplish?
What would success have looked like?
officeUse: PRD problem sections, roadmap narratives, and pitch decks.

title: Switch Interview
definition: A JTBD interview method exploring a customer's past decision to adopt or switch products.
example: You ask a Notion user: 'Tell me about when you moved your team wiki from Confluence — what triggered the first thought, and what almost stopped you?'
whenToUse: Deep discovery on why customers buy, churn, or choose competitors.
frameworkSteps: Explore first thought and trigger events
Document the hiring and firing moment
interviewPrompts: Tell me about the last time you switched from X to Y.
What were you using before? What changed?
officeUse: Win/loss analysis, churn research, and new market entry.

title: Job Map
definition: A breakdown of the steps a customer takes to complete a job, used to find innovation opportunities.
example: For 'plan a team offsite,' steps include define goals → shortlist venues → get budget approval → book — you find highest pain at budget approval and prioritize a cost-estimator feature.
whenToUse: Identifying underserved steps in a customer's workflow.
frameworkSteps: List job steps in chronological order
Rate pain and gain at each step
Prioritize steps with high pain and low satisfaction
interviewPrompts: Walk me through every step you took.
officeUse: Feature prioritization and blue-ocean opportunity finding.

title: Competing with Status Quo
definition: The insight that your biggest competitor is often doing nothing or a manual workaround, not another product.
example: Your expense tool competes with employees stuffing receipts in a shoebox and emailing finance once a month — not just Expensify.
whenToUse: Sizing opportunities and designing go-to-market strategy.
frameworkSteps: Quantify cost of status quo (time, money, risk)
Design solution that beats workaround, not just competitors
interviewPrompts: How do you handle this today without our product?
What would you do if our product didn't exist?
